content:
10 min read
Just now

--

There are two ways to find out whether someone sent you a message.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The first one is walking to the mailbox every minute and checking it manually.
The second one is letting the delivery person ring the bell when something actually arrives.
That is basically the difference between long polling and webhooks.
With long polling, your bot keeps asking Telegram:
Any updates?
What about now?
And now?
Telegram makes this less ridiculous by holding the connection open until an update appears or the timeout expires. That is why it is called long polling. But the important part does not change: your application is the one constantly initiating requests.
With webhooks, the model flips. You give Telegram a public HTTPS endpoint once, and Telegram sends a POST request to that endpoint whenever a new update arrives.
No messages, no requests.
No requests, no unnecessary work.
For a small pet bot with five users, polling is perfectly fine. It is simple, local-development friendly, and does not require a domain, TLS certificate, or reverse proxy.
But once your Telegram bot becomes part of a real system, polling starts to look less like simplicity and more like technical debt wearing a fake mustache.
Let’s move a Telegram bot from start_polling() to a production-style webhook setup using FastAPI, aiogram 3, Uvicorn, and Docker.
Why start_polling() Eventually Becomes a Problem
A classic tutorial bot usually looks like this:
await dp.start_polling(bot)One process. One event loop. One long-running polling cycle.
That is great for learning and prototypes. The trouble starts when the bot becomes important.
You cannot scale it horizontally
Telegram does not allow multiple consumers to call getUpdates with the same bot token at the same time.
If you start two polling instances, one of them will eventually receive something like:
Conflict: terminated by other getUpdates requestThat means your bot is stuck as a single-instance service.
One process.
One failure point.
One little gremlin sitting on your production architecture.
Deployments become downtime
With polling, you normally have one active process.
When you deploy a new version, you stop the old process and start the new one. During that window, the bot is not processing updates.
For small bots, that may be acceptable. For bots that handle orders, support tickets, lead generation, payments, or internal operations, that is not cute anymore.
The polling loop can die silently
Network timeout. Unexpected update payload. Unhandled exception. Temporary Telegram-side issue.
Sometimes the process is still alive, but the bot no longer consumes updates properly. From the outside, everything looks “up”, but users are staring at a dead chat.
That is the worst kind of outage: the one that pretends it is not an outage.
Idle bots still keep working
Even when nobody talks to the bot, the polling loop still exists. The connection still lives. The service still keeps asking for updates.
For one bot, this is noise.
For twenty or fifty bots on the same infrastructure, noise becomes a bill.
What Webhooks Change
With webhooks, your bot becomes a normal HTTP service.
Telegram sends a POST request to your endpoint when an update appears. Your FastAPI app receives that request, validates it, converts the payload into an aiogram Update, and passes it to the dispatcher.
The handlers stay almost exactly the same.
The transport changes.
The architecture improves.
A webhook-based bot can sit behind Nginx, Caddy, Traefik, Kubernetes Ingress, a load balancer, or any other boring production infrastructure that already knows how to deal with HTTP services.
That gives you:

• easier health checks;

• cleaner deployments;

• better observability;

• horizontal scaling behind a reverse proxy;

• no permanent polling loop;

• a much more standard production model.
The goal is simple: stop treating your bot like a script and start treating it like a service.
The Stack
We will use:
aiogram 3 Telegram bot framework
FastAPI HTTP API receiving Telegram webhook requests
Uvicorn ASGI server
Docker containerized deploymentThe project structure:
tg-webhook-bot/
├── app/
│ ├── __init__.py
│ ├── config.py
│ ├── handlers.py
│ └── main.py
├── requirements.txt
├── Dockerfile
├── docker-compose.yml
└── .env.exampleConfiguration
Secrets do not belong in source code.
The bot token, public webhook host, and Telegram secret token should come from environment variables.
# app/config.pyfrom functools import cached_property
from typing import Literal
from pydantic import Field, SecretStr
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict
class Settings(BaseSettings):
model_config = SettingsConfigDict(
env_file=".env",
env_file_encoding="utf-8",
extra="ignore",
)
bot_token: SecretStr = Field(alias="BOT_TOKEN")
webhook_host: str = Field(
alias="WEBHOOK_HOST",
description="Public HTTPS host, for example: https://bot.example.com",
)
webhook_path: str = Field(
default="/webhooks/telegram",
alias="WEBHOOK_PATH",
)
webhook_secret: SecretStr = Field(alias="WEBHOOK_SECRET")
drop_pending_updates: bool = Field(
default=False,
alias="DROP_PENDING_UPDATES",
)
delete_webhook_on_shutdown: bool = Field(
default=False,
alias="DELETE_WEBHOOK_ON_SHUTDOWN",
)
log_level: Literal["debug", "info", "warning", "error"] = Field(
default="info",
alias="LOG_LEVEL",
)
@cached_property
def webhook_url(self) -> str:
return f"{self.webhook_host.rstrip('/')}{self.webhook_path}"
settings = Settings()
There are two important flags here.
DROP_PENDING_UPDATES controls what happens to updates that accumulated while the bot was offline.
For a demo bot, setting it to true is fine. For a production bot that handles important commands, orders, forms, or support messages, you probably want false.
DELETE_WEBHOOK_ON_SHUTDOWN is intentionally false by default.
Why?
Because deleting the webhook during every shutdown can break blue-green deployments, rolling updates, or multi-instance deployments. Imagine a new container starts, registers the webhook, and then the old container stops and deletes it. Congratulations, production now has a rake on the floor.
For local development, setting it to true can be convenient. For production, be careful.
Bot Handlers
The handlers are normal aiogram handlers.
They do not know whether updates came from polling, webhooks, Kubernetes, Docker Compose, or a pigeon with a JSON envelope.
That is the whole point.
# app/handlers.pyfrom aiogram import Router
from aiogram.filters import CommandStart
from aiogram.types import Message
router = Router()
@router.message(CommandStart())
async def handle_start(message: Message) -> None:
await message.answer(
"Hey! I run on webhooks now, so I do not keep poking Telegram for updates."
)
@router.message()
async def echo_message(message: Message) -> None:
text = message.text or "I received your message, but there is no text to echo."
await message.answer(f"You said: {text}")
This is the part that should stay boring.
If moving from polling to webhooks forces you to rewrite business logic, something went wrong in your architecture.
FastAPI Application
Now comes the actual bridge between Telegram and aiogram.
FastAPI receives the POST request.
We check Telegram’s secret header.
Then we validate the JSON payload as an aiogram Update.
Finally, we pass it into the dispatcher using feed_update.
# app/main.pyimport secrets
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from typing import AsyncIterator
from aiogram import Bot, Dispatcher
from aiogram.client.default import DefaultBotProperties
from aiogram.enums import ParseMode
from aiogram.types import Update
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException, Request, Response, status
from app.config import settings
from app.handlers import router
@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI) -> AsyncIterator[None]:
bot = Bot(
token=settings.bot_token.get_secret_value(),
default=DefaultBotProperties(parse_mode=ParseMode.HTML),
)
dispatcher = Dispatcher()
dispatcher.include_router(router)
app.state.bot = bot
app.state.dispatcher = dispatcher
await bot.set_webhook(
url=settings.webhook_url,
secret_token=settings.webhook_secret.get_secret_value(),
allowed_updates=dispatcher.resolve_used_update_types(),
drop_pending_updates=settings.drop_pending_updates,
)
try:
yield
finally:
if settings.delete_webhook_on_shutdown:
await bot.delete_webhook(drop_pending_updates=False)
await bot.session.close()
def create_app() -> FastAPI:
app = FastAPI(
title="Telegram Webhook Bot",
version="1.0.0",
lifespan=lifespan,
)
@app.post(settings.webhook_path, include_in_schema=False)
async def telegram_webhook(request: Request) -> Response:
actual_secret = request.headers.get("X-Telegram-Bot-Api-Secret-Token", "")
expected_secret = settings.webhook_secret.get_secret_value()
if not secrets.compare_digest(actual_secret, expected_secret):
raise HTTPException(status_code=status.HTTP_403_FORBIDDEN)
payload = await request.json()
bot: Bot = request.app.state.bot
dispatcher: Dispatcher = request.app.state.dispatcher
update = Update.model_validate(payload, context={"bot": bot})
await dispatcher.feed_update(bot, update)
return Response(status_code=status.HTTP_200_OK)
@app.get("/health")
async def health() -> dict[str, str]:
return {"status": "ok"}
@app.get("/ready")
async def readiness(request: Request) -> dict[str, str]:
if not hasattr(request.app.state, "bot"):
raise HTTPException(status_code=status.HTTP_503_SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE)
return {"status": "ready"}
return app
A few details matter here.
Use secret_token
Your webhook endpoint is public. Random scanners, bots, and bored people with curl can hit it.
Telegram allows you to pass a secret_token when registering the webhook. Telegram then sends that value in the X-Telegram-Bot-Api-Secret-Token header.
If the header does not match, reject the request.
Also, use secrets.compare_digest() instead of plain string comparison. Is this overkill for a small Telegram bot? Maybe. Is it a good habit? Absolutely.
Use allowed_updates
This line is small but useful:
allowed_updates=dispatcher.resolve_used_update_types()It tells Telegram to send only the update types your dispatcher actually handles.
Less noise. Less unnecessary traffic. Fewer surprises.
Keep /health cheap
The health endpoint should not call Telegram.
It should answer whether the HTTP process is alive.
If you want a deeper check, add a separate diagnostic endpoint or metrics, but do not make the basic healthcheck depend on an external API. That is how you turn a temporary network hiccup into a container restart festival.
Dependencies
Keep versions pinned.
A production incident caused by a surprise dependency upgrade is a very stupid way to lose an evening.
# requirements.txtaiogram==3.29.0
fastapi==0.116.0
uvicorn[standard]==0.35.0
pydantic-settings==2.10.1
You can also use uv, Poetry, or pip-tools. The tool is not the point.
The point is reproducibility.
Environment Variables
Create an example file for the team.
Do not commit the real .env.
# .env.exampleBOT_TOKEN=1234567890:replace_with_real_token
WEBHOOK_HOST=https://bot.example.com
WEBHOOK_PATH=/webhooks/telegram
WEBHOOK_SECRET=replace_with_a_long_random_string
DROP_PENDING_UPDATES=false
DELETE_WEBHOOK_ON_SHUTDOWN=false
LOG_LEVEL=info
For local development with ngrok, WEBHOOK_HOST will be the temporary HTTPS URL from ngrok.
For production, it should be your real public domain.
Dockerfile
The container should be small, predictable, and not run as root.
# DockerfileFROM python:3.12-slim
ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 \
PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
PIP_NO_CACHE_DIR=1
WORKDIR /app
RUN addgroup --system app && adduser --system --ingroup app app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --upgrade pip && pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY --chown=app:app app ./app
USER app
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["uvicorn", "app.main:create_app", "--factory", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
The important bits:

• dependencies are installed before copying application code,
